attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the early pregnancy unit with vaginal bleeding and mild lower abdominal cramping for the past six hours. She is eight weeks pregnant by her last menstrual period. She has a history of three previous first-trimester miscarriages. She has had investigation following these miscarriages with no cause found. She has no other medical conditions and takes only pregnancy multivitamins.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 118/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her abdomen is soft with mild suprapubic tenderness. Speculum examination reveals a small amount of dark blood in the vaginal vault with a closed cervical os. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a single viable intrauterine pregnancy of 8 weeks and 2 days with fetal cardiac activity present. A small subchorionic haematoma is noted.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Serial serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with threatened miscarriage, characterised by vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy with a closed cervical os and ultrasound confirmation of fetal cardiac activity. Her history of recurrent miscarriage is significant in determining management.

The PRISM trial demonstrated that for the subgroup of pregnant women with two or more previous miscarriages and early pregnancy bleeding, vaginal progesterone supplementation probably increases the live birth rate and has little to no association with congenital abnormalities or severe adverse events. This was at a dose of 400 mg twice daily of micronised progesterone vaginally during the first trimester of pregnancy up to 16 weeks' gestation. There was no significant difference in women without the recurrent miscarriage history, thus use is not supported for the general population with threatened miscarriage. Vaginal administration is preferred as it provides better bioavailability and fewer side effects than oral administration.

Oral progesterone supplementation is less effective than vaginal administration due to first-pass metabolism and variable absorption. It also has more systemic side effects including drowsiness and nausea.

Clexane and aspirin are sometimes used in recurrent miscarriage when antiphospholipid syndrome is diagnosed. However, this patient has had investigations with no cause found, making empiric anticoagulation inappropriate.

Reassurance and expectant management alone would be reasonable in a patient with threatened miscarriage without a history of recurrent pregnancy loss. However, given this patient's history, active intervention with progesterone is warranted.

Serial serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements are not indicated when an intrauterine pregnancy with cardiac activity has been confirmed on ultrasound. They are more useful in cases where the location or viability of pregnancy is uncertain.

The patient should be advised to rest, avoid sexual intercourse until bleeding stops, and return if bleeding becomes heavy or pain increases. Follow-up ultrasound in 1-2 weeks is recommended to confirm ongoing viability. This event would also constitute as a potential sensitisation so if the women were Rhesus D negative, appropriate anti-D therapy should be considered.
learningPoints: Progesterone supplementation can reduce the risk of miscarriage in women with a history of recurrent pregnancy loss presenting with symptoms of a threatened miscarriage, however is not recommended for all cases of threatened miscarriage.
Transvaginal ultrasound showing fetal cardiac activity with bleeding indicates threatened miscarriage.
Serial beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements are not useful when an intrauterine pregnancy is visible on ultrasound.
references: Regan L, Rai R, Saravelos S, Li TC. Recurrent Miscarriage: Green-top Guideline No. 17. BJOG [Internet]. 2023 Jun 19 [cited 2024 Nov 27]; Available from: https://doi.org/10.1111/1471-0528.17515

Coomarasamy A, Devall AJ, Cheed V, Harb H, Middleton LJ, Gallos ID, et al. A randomized trial of progesterone in women with bleeding in early pregnancy. N Engl J Med [Internet]. 2019 May 8 [cited 2024 Nov 27];380(19):1815-24. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1813730

RANZCOG. Miscarriage, Recurrent Miscarriage and Ectopic Pregnancy (C-Gyn 38) Clinical Guideline [Internet]. 2025 [cited 2025 May 25]; Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Miscarriage-Ectopic-Pregnancy.pdf
